title: Extract shared sculptor test helpers from sculptor_group_{c,d}_tests.rs
description:
## Problem

`tests/sculptor_group_c_tests.rs` and `tests/sculptor_group_d_tests.rs`
both define functionally identical helper scaffolding (38 LOC each):

- `load_rule(name: &str) -> AstRule` — TOML path + AstRule::from_path
- `matches(rule: &AstRule, src: &str) -> usize` — ExecutionContext + execute
- `expect_match(rule, src, label)` — assert count > 0 with formatted message
- `expect_no_match(rule, src, label)` — assert count == 0 with formatted message

`tests/sculptor_group_d_tests.rs` lines 11-48 are line-for-line identical
to `tests/sculptor_group_c_tests.rs` lines 8-45. Any future sculptor group
port (or maintenance of these helpers, e.g. switching to ParserCache) will
have to be done in N places.

## Scope

In `tests/` only:

1. Create `tests/sculptor_common.rs` containing the four helpers (`load_rule`,
   `matches`, `expect_match`, `expect_no_match`) gated behind
   `#![cfg(feature = "lang-python")]`.
2. Update `tests/sculptor_group_c_tests.rs` to declare `mod sculptor_common;`
   and use the shared helpers; delete the inline copies.
3. Update `tests/sculptor_group_d_tests.rs` to do the same.

Follow the existing `tests/common.rs` precedent (already used by
`foundation_tests.rs` via `mod common;`).

## Constraints

- Touch only files under `tests/` (1 new file, 2 modified).
- All tests must continue to pass with the same assertions.
- Net diff should remove ~30 LOC (delete ~76 lines of duplication, add
  ~46 lines of helper module + use statements).

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` passes (no warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` passes (all 687 tests, no count change)
- `cargo run --release -- check` passes
- The diff is contained to `tests/sculptor_common.rs`,
  `tests/sculptor_group_c_tests.rs`, and `tests/sculptor_group_d_tests.rs`

## Anti-goals

- Do NOT consolidate into `tests/common.rs`. That file is feature-agnostic;
  sculptor helpers depend on `feature = "lang-python"` and `AstRule`,
  and would force a feature gate into common.rs.
- Do NOT refactor the actual test bodies (the `#[test]` functions) — only
  factor out the four shared helpers.
- Do NOT generalize beyond what's currently shared (e.g. don't introduce a
  trait for "rule executors" — the four functions are tightly scoped to
  AstRule and Python).

## Future-proofing

If sculptor Groups E+ get added later (none currently planned), they'll
naturally pick up the shared module via `mod sculptor_common;` — adding a
new test file becomes a 1-line scaffold instead of a 38-line copy.
close_reason: Landed in 4877de7. Helpers hoisted to tests/sculptor_common.rs; tests/sculptor_group_{c,d}_tests.rs each shrink by 35 lines and gain a 3-line mod+use scaffold. 687 tests still pass.

title: Restructure builtin-ratchets directory: language-first organization
description:
## Goal
Restructure the builtin-ratchets directory to use language-first organization in preparation for adding multiple language-specific ratchets.

## Current Structure
```
builtin-ratchets/
├── ast/
│   ├── python/
│   │   └── no-bare-except.toml
│   ├── rust/
│   │   └── no-unwrap.toml, no-panic.toml, no-expect.toml
│   └── typescript/
│       └── no-any.toml
└── regex/
    └── no-todo-comments.toml, no-fixme-comments.toml
```

## Target Structure
```
builtin-ratchets/
├── python/
│   ├── ast/
│   │   └── no-bare-except.toml
│   └── regex/
│       └── (Python-specific regex rules go here)
├── rust/
│   ├── ast/
│   │   └── no-unwrap.toml, no-panic.toml, no-expect.toml
│   └── regex/
├── typescript/
│   ├── ast/
│   │   └── no-any.toml
│   └── regex/
└── common/
    └── regex/
        └── no-todo-comments.toml, no-fixme-comments.toml
```

## Implementation Notes

title: Port Python regex ratchets from test_ratchets.py
description:
## Goal
Port the Python-specific regex-based ratchets from ratchets/test_ratchets.py to builtin Python ratchets.

## Prerequisites
- Bead code-41: Directory restructure to language-first organization
- Bead code-44: Reusable exclusion patterns with @reference syntax

## Source Reference
The ratchets are defined in: ratchets/test_ratchets.py

## Ratchets to Port (Regex-based)

Each ratchet should become a TOML file in builtin-ratchets/python/regex/ with:
- [rule] section: id, description, severity
- [match] section: pattern, languages = ["python"]

### Security ratchets (severity = "error"):
1. **no-exec-usage**: pattern = \`\\bexec\\s*\\(\` - "exec() should not be used due to security and maintainability concerns"
2. **no-eval-usage**: pattern = \`\\beval\\s*\\(\` - "eval() should not be used due to security and maintainability concerns"

### Exception handling ratchets:
3. **no-bare-except**: pattern = \`except\\s*:\` - "Bare 'except:' catches all exceptions. Specify exception types" (moved from AST)
4. **no-broad-exception**: pattern = \`except\\s+Exception\\b\` - "Catching 'Exception' is too broad. Use specific exception types"
5. **no-base-exception**: pattern = \`except\\s+BaseException\\b\` - "Catching 'BaseException' catches system exits. Use specific types"

### Import style ratchets:
6. **no-inline-imports**: pattern = \`(?m)^[ \\t]+import\\s+\\w+|^[ \\t]+from\\s+\\S+\\s+import\\b\` - "Imports should be at the top of the file"
7. **no-relative-imports**: pattern = \`(?m)^from\\s+\\.\` - "Always use absolute imports, never relative"
8. **no-import-datetime**: pattern = \`(?m)^import datetime$\` - "Import specific items instead"

### Banned libraries ratchets:
9. **no-asyncio-import**: pattern = \`\\bimport\\s+asyncio\\b|\\bfrom\\s+asyncio\\b\` - "asyncio is banned. Use synchronous code"
10. **no-pandas-import**: pattern = \`\\bimport\\s+pandas\\b|\\bfrom\\s+pandas\\b\` - "pandas is banned. Use polars instead"
11. **no-dataclasses-import**: pattern = \`\\bimport\\s+dataclasses\\b|\\bfrom\\s+dataclasses\\b\` - "Use pydantic models instead"
12. **no-yaml-usage**: pattern = \`yaml\` - "NEVER use YAML. Use TOML for configuration"

### Code patterns ratchets:
13. **no-while-true**: pattern = \`\\bwhile\\s+True\\s*:\` - "Use explicit conditions instead"
14. **no-namedtuple-usage**: pattern = \`\\bnamedtuple\\s*\\(\` - "Use pydantic models instead"
15. **no-global-keyword**: pattern = \`\\bglobal\\s+\\w+\` - "Pass state explicitly through function parameters"
16. **no-time-sleep**: pattern = \`\\btime\\.sleep\\s*\\(|\\bfrom\\s+time\\s+import\\s+sleep\\b\` - "Use polling instead"
17. **no-bare-print**: pattern = \`(?m)^\\s*print\\s*\\(\` - "Use logger instead"
18. **no-click-echo**: pattern = \`\\bclick\\.echo\\b|\\bfrom\\s+click\\s+import\\s+.*\\becho\\b\` - "Use logger.info() instead"

### Type annotation ratchets:
19. **no-bare-generic-types**: pattern = \`:\\s*(list|dict|tuple|set|List|Dict|Tuple|Set|Mapping|Sequence)\\s*($|[,\\)\\]])\` - "Specify type parameters"
20. **no-typing-builtin-imports**: pattern = \`\\bfrom\\s+typing\\s+import\\s+.*\\b(Dict|List|Set|Tuple)\\b\` - "Use lowercase builtins"
21. **no-literal-multi-options**: pattern = \`Literal\\[.*,.*\\]\` - "Create an UpperCaseStrEnum instead"

### Docstring ratchets:
22. **no-init-docstrings**: pattern = \`(?m)def __init__[^:]*:\\s+"""\` - "Class docstring should describe the class"
23. **no-args-in-docstrings**: pattern = \`"""[\\s\\S]{0,500}Args:\` - "Use inline parameter comments"
24. **no-returns-in-docstrings**: pattern = \`"""[\\s\\S]{0,500}Returns:\` - "Use inline return type comments"
25. **no-trailing-comments**: pattern = \`[^\\s#].*[ \\t]#(?!\\s*ty:\\s*ignore\\[)\` - "Comments should be on own line"

### Naming ratchets:
26. **no-num-prefix**: pattern = \`\\bnum_\\w+|\\bnumOf|\\bnum[A-Z]\` - "Use 'count' or 'idx' instead"

### Exception raising ratchets:
27. **no-builtin-exception-raises**: pattern = \`raise\\s+(ValueError|KeyError|TypeError|AttributeError|IndexError|RuntimeError|OSError|IOError)\\(\` - "Create custom exception types"

### Logging ratchets:
28. **no-fstring-logging**: pattern = \`logger\\.(trace|debug|info|warning|error|exception)\\(f\` - "Use loguru-style placeholder syntax"

## Implementation Notes
- Use Rust regex syntax (escape backslashes appropriately in TOML)
- Use (?m) flag for multiline patterns where needed
- Set severity = "error" for security ratchets, "warning" for style ratchets
- All rules should have languages = ["python"]
- Test each regex pattern compiles and matches expected patterns

## Definition of Done
- All 28 regex ratchets are created as TOML files
- All rules load successfully via load_builtin_regex_rules()
- All tests pass
- cargo clippy passes

title: Port Python AST ratchets from test_ratchets.py
description:
## Goal
Port the Python-specific AST-based ratchets from ratchets/test_ratchets.py to builtin Python ratchets using tree-sitter queries.

## Prerequisites
- Bead code-41: Directory restructure to language-first organization
- Bead code-44: Reusable exclusion patterns with @reference syntax

## Source Reference
The ratchets are defined in: ratchets/test_ratchets.py
These use custom Python functions that do AST analysis.

## Design Decisions (Resolved)

**Decision 1**: no-bare-except → **Convert to regex rule** (moved to code-42)
The regex `except\s*:` is simpler and more precise than the AST approach.

**Decision 2**: Post-filtering in Rust → **Approved**
For rules requiring negative matching (like no-init-in-non-exception-classes), implement post-filter logic in Rust after tree-sitter returns matches.

**Decision 3**: Test file exclusion → **Use @reference syntax**
Rules will use `exclude = "@python_tests"` referencing patterns defined in ratchet.toml (implemented in code-44).

## Ratchets to Port (AST-based)

Each ratchet should become a TOML file in builtin-ratchets/python/ast/ with:
- [rule] section: id, description, severity
- [match] section: language = "python", query = "..."

### 1. no-if-elif-without-else
Original: find_if_elif_without_else()
Description: "All if/elif chains must have an else clause to ensure all cases are handled explicitly"

Tree-sitter query: Match if_statement with elif_clause children but no else_clause.

### 2. no-inline-functions
Original: find_inline_functions()
Description: "Functions should not be defined inside other functions in non-test code"

Tree-sitter query: Match function_definition that is a descendant of another function_definition.
Exclude: @python_tests

### 3. no-underscore-imports
Original: find_underscore_imports()
Description: "Do not import underscore-prefixed functions/classes/constants in non-test code"

Tree-sitter query: Match import where the imported name starts with underscore.
Exclude: @python_tests

### 4. no-init-in-non-exception-classes
Original: find_init_methods_in_non_exception_classes()
Description: "Do not define __init__ methods in non-Exception/Error classes. Use Pydantic models instead"

Tree-sitter query: Match class_definition containing __init__ method.
Post-filter: In Rust, filter out matches where class name ends in "Exception" or "Error".
Exclude: @python_tests

## Implementation Notes
- Use tree-sitter-python query syntax
- Test queries against sample Python code
- Implement post-filter support in AstRule for rule 4

## Definition of Done
- All 4 AST ratchets are created as TOML files (3 remaining after no-bare-except moved to regex)
- Post-filter logic implemented for no-init-in-non-exception-classes
- All rules load and execute correctly
- All tests pass
- cargo clippy passes

title: Add reusable exclusion patterns with @reference syntax
description:
## Goal
Add support for defining reusable glob patterns in ratchet.toml that can be referenced in rule files using @name syntax.

## Design

### ratchet.toml addition
```toml
[ratchet]
version = "1"

[patterns]
python_tests = ["**/test_*.py", "**/*_test.py", "**/tests/**", "**/conftest.py"]
rust_tests = ["**/tests/**", "**/benches/**"]
generated = ["**/generated/**", "**/vendor/**"]
```

### Rule file usage
```toml
[rule]
id = "no-exec-usage"
description = "exec() should not be used"
severity = "error"

[match]
pattern = "\\bexec\\s*\\("
languages = ["python"]
exclude = "@python_tests"  # @ prefix references pattern from ratchet.toml
```

## Implementation

### 1. Update Config struct (src/config/ratchet_toml.rs)
Add patterns field to Config:
```rust
pub struct Config {
    pub version: String,
    pub languages: Vec<Language>,
    pub include: Vec<GlobPattern>,
    pub exclude: Vec<GlobPattern>,
    pub rules: RuleConfig,
    pub output: OutputConfig,
    pub patterns: HashMap<String, Vec<GlobPattern>>,  // NEW
}
```

### 2. Update rule loading to accept pattern context
Both RegexRule::from_toml and AstRule::from_toml need access to the patterns map to resolve @references.

Options:
a) Add a second parameter: `from_toml(content: &str, patterns: &HashMap<String, Vec<GlobPattern>>)`
b) Add a resolve step after parsing: `rule.resolve_patterns(patterns)`
c) Use a context struct: `from_toml(content: &str, ctx: &RuleContext)`

Recommend option (c) for extensibility.

### 3. Pattern resolution logic
In the match section parsing:
- If exclude/include value starts with @, look up in patterns map
- If not found, return error: "Unknown pattern reference: @foo"
- If found, expand to the glob list
- If value doesn't start with @, treat as literal glob (existing behavior)

### 4. Support both single reference and mixed lists
```toml
exclude = "@python_tests"                    # Single reference
exclude = ["@python_tests", "src/legacy/**"] # Mixed: reference + literal
```

### 5. Update documentation
- DESIGN.md: Document the [patterns] section
- README.md: Add example in Configuration section

## Files to Modify
- src/config/ratchet_toml.rs - Add patterns to Config struct
- src/rules/regex_rule.rs - Accept patterns context, resolve @references
- src/rules/ast/ast_rule.rs - Accept patterns context, resolve @references  
- src/rules/registry.rs - Pass patterns context to rule loading
- src/cli/check.rs - Pass config.patterns to rule loading
- DESIGN.md - Document feature
- README.md - Add usage example

## Test Cases
1. Pattern defined and referenced correctly → resolves to glob list
2. Pattern referenced but not defined → clear error message
3. Mixed list with @reference and literal glob → both work
4. No patterns section in config → empty map, @references error
5. Backward compatibility: existing configs without [patterns] still work

## Definition of Done
- [patterns] section parses correctly in ratchet.toml
- @reference syntax resolves in rule exclude/include fields
- Clear error messages for undefined pattern references
- All existing tests pass
- New tests cover pattern resolution
- Documentation updated

title: Design: Exclude non-program files from ratchet violation checking
description:
## Problem

Ratchet currently checks all files matching include patterns, including non-program files like:
- .beads/issues.jsonl (task metadata)
- ratchet-counts.toml (configuration)
- DESIGN.md, ARCHITECTURE.md (documentation)
- builtin-ratchets/*.toml (rule definitions that mention patterns like FIXME)

Rules like no-todo-comments and no-fixme-comments are designed for program source code, not metadata or documentation.

## Requirements

1. Define what constitutes a program file vs non-program file
2. Design a mechanism to automatically exclude non-program files from comment-based rules
3. Consider whether this should be:
   - Rule-level configuration (rules declare what file types they apply to)
   - Global configuration (ratchet.toml specifies program file patterns)
   - Language-based (only check files with recognized language extensions)
4. Ensure backward compatibility with existing configurations
5. Document the design decision

## Success Criteria

- Running cargo run -- check should not report violations in .beads/, *.md, or *.toml files for comment-based rules
- The solution should be generalizable to other non-code-quality rules

title: Remove unwrap() from ast_rule.rs production code
description:
## Problem

## Locations

2. Line 318: `parser.language().unwrap()` in `validate_query()`

## Proposed Solutions

**Line 234 - Use early return pattern:**
```rust
let tree_sitter_lang = match parser.language() {
    Some(lang) => lang,
    None => return vec![], // Parser not properly configured
};
```

**Line 318 - Use ok_or_else (function already returns Result):**
```rust
let tree_sitter_lang = parser.language().ok_or_else(|| {
    RuleError::InvalidQuery("Parser language not configured".to_string())
})?;
```

## Success Criteria
- No unwrap() in production code paths
- All tests pass
- cargo clippy passes

title: Remove unwrap() from executor.rs production code
Design: Idiomatic test patterns to eliminate unwrap/expect
description:
## Problem

The test suite contains ~500+ unwrap/expect calls across 12 test files. While STYLE_GUIDE.md allows these in tests, there are more idiomatic patterns that provide:
- Better failure messages
- Cleaner code
- Consistent patterns across the codebase

## Analysis Summary

| File | unwrap/expect count |
|------|---------------------|
| e2e_integration_tests.rs | 120 |
| engine_integration_tests.rs | 94 |
| cli_integration_tests.rs | 73 |
| ast_integration_tests.rs | 55 |
| scalability_tests.rs | 38 |
| check_command_tests.rs | 34 |
| file_walker_tests.rs | 29 |
| foundation_tests.rs | 24 |
| config_tests.rs | 23 |
| regex_rules_tests.rs | 4 |

## Recommended Patterns

1. **Tests returning Result<(), E>** - Use ? operator instead of unwrap
2. **let-else pattern** - `let Some(x) = opt else { panic!("message"); };`
3. **Helper macros** - Create assert_ok!, assert_some! macros
4. **Result-returning helpers** - Refactor setup_* functions to return Result

## Implementation Plan

1. Create tests/common/mod.rs with TestResult type and helper macros
2. Convert helper functions to return Result
3. Migrate test files incrementally (smallest first)
4. Consider adding anyhow to dev-dependencies

- Maintain test coverage

title: Collapse repetitive load blocks in src/rules/builtin.rs into helpers
description:
## Problem

`src/rules/builtin.rs` has accreted six nearly-identical load blocks across
`load_builtin_regex_rules` (3 blocks) and `load_builtin_ast_rules` (3 blocks).
Each new per-language array (e.g. `BUILTIN_TYPESCRIPT_REGEX_RULES` added in
code-aoe) is being wired by copy-pasting a ~10-line `for ... in ARRAY { ... }`
block whose only differences from the others are:

1. The constant being iterated
2. The label text in the `RuleError::InvalidDefinition` message
3. The `#[cfg(feature = "lang-X")]` gate

The Group C and Group D AST porting beads (code-gkp, code-m2l) will continue
this pattern, and any future language (Go, etc.) will copy a new block as well.

## Scope

In `src/rules/builtin.rs` only:

1. Introduce two private helpers:
   - `fn extend_regex_rules(rules: &mut RuleList, source: &[(&str, &str)], label: &str) -> Result<(), RuleError>`
   - `fn extend_ast_rules(rules: &mut RuleList, source: &[(&str, &str)], rule_context: &RuleContext, label: &str) -> Result<(), RuleError>`
2. Rewrite `load_builtin_regex_rules` and `load_builtin_ast_rules` so each
   per-language block becomes a single `#[cfg(...)] extend_*_rules(...)?;`
   call site.
3. Keep all `#[cfg(feature = "lang-*")]` gates at the call site (so the static
   constants stay feature-gated as before).
4. Preserve the exact error message format ("Failed to parse built-in {label}
   rule '{name}': {err}") so existing error path tests still pass.

## Constraints

- Touch only `src/rules/builtin.rs`.
- No behavior change: iteration order, error texts, public API unchanged.
- Net diff should remove ~30 lines (deleting ~66 lines, adding ~36 lines).

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` passes (no warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` passes (existing `test_load_builtin_regex_rules`,
  `test_load_builtin_ast_rules`, and the validity tests must pass with
  the same counts as today)
- `cargo run --release -- check` passes
- The diff is contained to `src/rules/builtin.rs`

## Anti-goals

- Do NOT change the per-language `BUILTIN_*` constant layout. Adding TOMLs
  to those arrays must remain the one-line-per-rule operation it is today.
- Do NOT centralize the feature gating into the helper — keep gates visible
  at call sites so dead-code removal under `--no-default-features` builds
  remains obvious.

title: Display region names in check command output
description:
## Problem

When running `cargo run -- check`, the summary output shows multiple lines for the same rule without indicating which region each line refers to:

```
no-expect: 7 violations (budget: 7) ✓
no-expect: 8 violations (budget: 8) ✓
```

This is confusing because it appears as if the same rule is being checked multiple times. In reality, each line represents a different region (directory subtree) with its own budget.

## Root Cause

In `src/output/human.rs`, the summary section outputs `status.rule_id` but not `status.region`. The region data exists in the `RuleRegionStatus` struct but is not displayed.

## Proposed Fix

Update the output format to include the region name:

**Current:**
```
✓ no-expect: 7 violations (budget: 7)
```

**Proposed:**
```
✓ no-expect [src/cli]: 7 violations (budget: 7)
```

## Files to Modify
- `src/output/human.rs` - Add region to summary output format
- `src/output/jsonl.rs` - Verify region is included in JSONL output (likely already there)

## Success Criteria
- Region name is displayed in human-readable output
- Tests pass
- Output is clear and unambiguous

title: Convert Python regex rules to AST: no-base-exception, no-broad-exception
description:
## Goal
Convert the following Python regex rules to AST-based rules to minimize false positives from string literals:
- no-base-exception (currently: except\\s+BaseException\\b)
- no-broad-exception (currently: except\\s+Exception\\b)

## Approach
Use tree-sitter Python queries to match except_clause nodes with specific exception types.

**IMPORTANT**: The query must handle BOTH forms:
1. `except BaseException:` - exception is direct identifier child
2. `except BaseException as e:` - exception is inside as_pattern

Correct query structure:
```scm
(except_clause
  [
    (identifier) @exception
    (as_pattern (identifier) @exception)
  ]
  (#eq? @exception "BaseException")) @violation
```

## Files to modify
- Create: builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-base-exception.toml
- Create: builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-broad-exception.toml
- Delete: builtin-ratchets/python/regex/no-base-exception.toml
- Delete: builtin-ratchets/python/regex/no-broad-exception.toml

## Acceptance
- Tests pass for new AST rules
- No false positives on strings like "except BaseException is bad"
- Violations detected on actual except clauses (both with and without as clause)

title: Convert Python regex rules to AST: no-eval-usage, no-exec-usage

- no-eval-usage (currently: \beval\s*\()
- no-exec-usage (currently: \bexec\s*\()

## Approach
Use tree-sitter Python queries to match call expressions with eval/exec as the function:
```scm
(call
  function: (identifier) @func
  (#eq? @func "eval")) @violation
```

title: Convert Python regex rules to AST: no-while-true, no-global-keyword, no-bare-print
description:
## Goal
Convert the following Python regex rules to AST-based rules to minimize false positives:
- no-while-true (currently: \bwhile\s+True\s*:)
- no-global-keyword (currently: \bglobal\s+\w+)
- no-bare-print (currently: (?m)^\s*print\s*\()

## Approach
Use tree-sitter Python queries:

### no-while-true
```scm
(while_statement
  condition: (true)) @violation
```

### no-global-keyword
```scm
(global_statement) @violation
```

### no-bare-print
```scm
(expression_statement
  (call
    function: (identifier) @func
    (#eq? @func "print"))) @violation
```

## Files to modify

title: Create Python AST rules for TODO/FIXME comments
description:
## Goal

- "This is a TODO in a string" - false positive
- # TODO: fix this - correct match

## Approach
Create Python AST rules that match only comment nodes:
```scm
(comment) @comment
(#match? @comment "(?i)\\bTODO\\b")
```

Note: Keep the common/regex rules as fallback for languages without AST support.
Python-specific rules will override them due to the loading order.

title: Port sculptor's path-scoped regex ratchet rules (Group B)
description:
Port the 10 sculptor rules in Group B of docs/supporting-sculptor.md. Each is a regex (no lookaround) plus an `include` glob restricting it to a specific subtree — sculptor's production code, integration tests, or frontend TSX.

Rules in scope (sculptor names):
sculptor_copytree_calls, sculptor_subprocess_calls, integration_test_page_reload, integration_test_non_testid_queries, integration_test_css_locators, integration_test_type_method, integration_test_page_goto, integration_test_page_evaluate, integration_test_time_sleep, raw_html_button_in_tsx.

Most are Python integration-test rules scoped to `sculptor/tests/integration/**/*.py`. `raw_html_button_in_tsx` is TypeScript (goes under `builtin-ratchets/typescript/regex/`).

Caveat on `sculptor_subprocess_calls`: sculptor expresses its path filter with a regex negative lookahead (`(?!.*(?:test|testing|scripts)/.*|.*_test\.py$)`). Globs can't express negative lookahead — translate to `include = ["sculptor/sculptor/**/*.py"]` plus `exclude = ["**/test/**", "**/testing/**", "**/scripts/**", "**/*_test.py"]`. The doc spells this out.

Acceptance:
- 10 new TOML files (9 python/regex, 1 typescript/regex).
- cargo nextest run / cargo clippy / cargo fmt --check all pass.

title: Investigate no-underscore-imports overcount vs sculptor (delta +62)
description:
Sculptor's `import_underscore` counts 4 violations. Our existing `no-underscore-imports` counts 66 on the same codebase. Delta +62 — the largest remaining gap.

## Sculptor's rule

`imbue_core/imbue_core/ratchets/ratchet_rules.py`, name `import_underscore`:

```python
regex=re.compile(r"^(from [\w.]+ )?import __?\w+"),
exclude_test_files=True,
```

In English: catches `import _xxx` and `import __xxx` (one or two leading underscores), plus the same shapes in `from yyy import __?...`. The matched thing is the THING BEING IMPORTED. Module names are not constrained — `from _module import foo` does NOT match.

## Our rule

`builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-underscore-imports.toml`:

```toml
query = """
(import_from_statement
  (aliased_import
    name: (dotted_name
      (identifier) @name
      (#match? @name "^_"))) @violation)

(import_from_statement
  (dotted_name
    (identifier) @name
    (#match? @name "^_")) @violation)
"""
exclude = "@python_tests"
```

The first pattern matches `from x import _y as z` (aliased) where the imported name starts with `_`. Fine.

The second pattern matches `import_from_statement` containing a `(dotted_name (identifier))` whose identifier starts with `_`. **This is structurally ambiguous**: tree-sitter Python represents `from foo import _bar` as approximately `(import_from_statement (dotted_name (identifier "foo")) (dotted_name (identifier "_bar")))`. Both the module path AND the imported name appear as direct `(dotted_name)` children. Our query matches EITHER — including `from _x import y` (legal in Python; sculptor doesn't flag it).

Also: our rule doesn't catch direct `import _x` statements (the `import_statement` node, not `import_from_statement`). Sculptor catches those.

## Hypothesis

The +62 gap is dominated by our query catching `from _module import ...` cases that sculptor doesn't intend to catch. These are common in Python codebases that have `_internal` packages — sculptor likely has many `from _xxx import ...` cases.

Net effect: our query is wrong in TWO directions:
- Catches `from _module import ...` — sculptor wouldn't flag → contributes overcount
- Misses bare `import _foo` — sculptor would flag → contributes undercount

The +62 is the net; the underlying mis-shape is bigger than that.

## Investigation

1. Read `builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-underscore-imports.toml` and study the tree-sitter shape of `import_from_statement` (use `tree-sitter parse` or write a tiny test).
2. Run our pilot against sculptor and dump per-violation records with file paths and line snippets.
3. Categorize the 66 violations:
   - How many are `from _module import foo` (false positives — sculptor wouldn't catch)?
   - How many are `from foo import _bar` (legitimate, sculptor catches)?
4. Use sculptor's `get_most_recently_broken_ratchets.py` to dump sculptor's 4 violations. Confirm they're all `from x import _y` or `import _y` shape.
5. Fix the query to:
   - Match `from X import _Y` (imported name starts with `_`)
   - Match `import _Y` (direct import of a `_`-prefixed module — sculptor catches this via `import _\w+`)
   - NOT match `from _X import Y` (module path starts with `_` is a separate concern)

## Acceptance

EITHER:
- Rewrite the query to match the correct shapes. Re-run pilot — counts should converge near sculptor's 4. Add tests in `tests/sculptor_group_c_tests.rs` (or wherever the existing `no-underscore-imports` is tested) covering all three cases.
- OR: Argue that flagging `from _module import ...` IS desirable (importing from a private module is a legit style concern) and the rule should be split into two: one for imported-name and one for module-name. In that case, file follow-up beads for the split, restore parity-with-sculptor for the imported-name rule, and document the new rule.

`cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy`, `cargo nextest run`, `cargo run --release -- check` all pass. Atomic commit(s), trailer `Co-authored-by: Sculptor <sculptor@imbue.com>`.

Do NOT touch `.beads/` — coordinator handles bead state.

title: Port sculptor's missing ratchet rules to builtin TOMLs
description:

title: Port sculptor's lookaround ratchet rules as tree-sitter AST (Group C)
description:
Port the 10 sculptor rules in Group C of docs/supporting-sculptor.md as tree-sitter AST rules under `builtin-ratchets/python/ast/`. Each rule's original Python regex uses lookbehind `(?<!...)` or lookahead `(?!...)` to assert that a construct does or doesn't appear in a particular syntactic context. Rust's `regex` crate doesn't compile those; the equivalent intent is expressed structurally via tree-sitter queries on the Python grammar.

Rules in scope (sculptor names):
attrs, args_kwargs, non_sys_exit, non_build_classmethods, non_private_staticmethods, mutable_attr_in_frozen_dataclass, unnumbered_pyre_ignore, unnumbered_pyre_fixme, unlabeled_type_ignore, cast.

Tree-sitter 0.22's standard `#eq?`, `#match?`, `#not-match?` predicates are already used by the existing builtin AST rules — `builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-eval-usage.toml` and `no-underscore-imports.toml` are reasonable shape templates.

The doc gives each rule a one-paragraph description, the sculptor regex (for reference), and a rough tree-sitter approach. The implementing agent should resolve exact query shapes and validate against sculptor's `match_examples` / `non_match_examples`.

Acceptance:
- 10 new TOML files under `builtin-ratchets/python/ast/`.

title: Support distributed .ratchetignore files for per-directory exclusions
description:
Sculptor's Python ratchets honors gitignore-style exclude files named `ratchet_excluded.txt` placed anywhere in the repo tree. Patterns in each file are matched relative to that file's directory, same semantics as `.gitignore`. This is how sculptor excludes things like `notebook/` and `quarantine/` subdirectories from ratchet enforcement, locally to those parts of the tree.

This library currently only supports a top-level `exclude = [...]` glob list in `ratchets.toml`. There is no mechanism for distributed ignore files.

Add `.ratchetignore` support so that sculptor (and other downstream users) can keep their per-directory exclusions in-place during migration:

- `src/engine/file_walker.rs` already uses `ignore::WalkBuilder`. Add `add_custom_ignore_filename(".ratchetignore")` so the `ignore` crate treats `.ratchetignore` files the same way it treats `.gitignore` and `.ignore`.
- Confirm: nested `.ratchetignore` files compose correctly (a deeper one overrides a shallower one for the same pattern, per gitignore semantics).
- Integration test under `tests/` that exercises a multi-level `.ratchetignore` tree (root-level, subdirectory, negation pattern).
- Mention the file in README.md and DESIGN.md as a public-facing config mechanism alongside `ratchets.toml` and `ratchet-counts.toml`.

This unblocks sculptor's migration off its `ratchet_excluded.txt` files. Without it, sculptor would have to flatten every distributed exclude into top-level globs in `ratchets.toml` — feasible but ugly and loses the locality benefit.

Acceptance:
- `.ratchetignore` files placed anywhere in the tree are honored by file discovery.
- New integration test passes.
- README.md and DESIGN.md mention the feature.
- cargo nextest run / cargo clippy / cargo fmt --check all pass.

title: Make builtin rule count assertions parametric to avoid mechanical churn
description:
## Problem

The test_load_builtin_regex_rules unit test in src/rules/builtin.rs (and
similarly test_load_builtin_ast_rules) asserts the total count of loaded
rules with a hardcoded number that has been bumped three times in two days:

- Initial: 22
- After Group A (code-61d): 22 -> 43
- After Group B (code-aoe): 43 -> 53 (split into +50 Python and +1 TypeScript)

This is mechanical churn that every new builtin rule port forces. Every
upcoming bead (code-gkp, code-m2l, future C/D follow-ups) will have to
edit this assertion to a new magic number, exposing the test to silent
copy-paste bugs (rule registered but not counted, or vice versa).

## Recommendation

Replace the hardcoded total with a parametric assertion that derives from
the same constants the loader iterates. Two viable approaches:

Approach A: Assert equality against the constants directly. Sum
BUILTIN_REGEX_RULES.len() plus per-feature BUILTIN_*_REGEX_RULES.len()
inside the matching cfg blocks, then assert_eq! against rules.len().

This makes the test additive: a new TOML registered in the array updates
the expected count automatically. The test still catches the case where
a rule is added to the array but fails to load.

Approach B: Drop the total-count assertion and rely on the presence-check
assertions (assert! rule_ids.contains) plus a separate sanity test that
loads each constant in isolation.

Approach A is preferred (smaller change, preserves the spirit of the test).

## Scope

In src/rules/builtin.rs cfg(test) mod tests block only:

1. Replace the hardcoded expected counts in test_load_builtin_regex_rules
   with sums of BUILTIN_*_REGEX_RULES.len() per feature gate.
2. Do the same for test_load_builtin_ast_rules (currently uses
   assert! rules.len() >= 5 which is too loose).

## Constraints

- Touch only src/rules/builtin.rs.
- All existing presence checks (assert! rule_ids.contains ...) must
  remain - those are the load-bearing assertions about which rules exist.
- No public API changes.

## Definition of Done

- cargo nextest run passes
- Adding a new TOML to e.g. BUILTIN_PYTHON_REGEX_RULES requires NO test
  edit (verify by mentally walking through code-gkp expected diff)
- cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features pass
close_reason: Replaced hardcoded total-count assertions in src/rules/builtin.rs with sums of BUILTIN_*_RULES.len() per feature gate. Adding a new TOML now requires no test edit. Commit ce63f4a.

title: Port sculptor's match-exhaustiveness ratchet rule (Group D)
description:
Port `match_without_wildcard_or_assert_never` (Group D of docs/supporting-sculptor.md) from sculptor's `MatchCaseRatchetTest`. Sculptor implements this with hand-coded indentation parsing; with tree-sitter the rule collapses to matching `match_statement` and using a `#not-match?` predicate against the captured node text to require `case _ as <var>: assert_never(<var>)` somewhere in the block.

Target: `builtin-ratchets/python/ast/match-must-assert-never.toml`.

Caveats (called out in the doc):
1. Rust's regex (used by tree-sitter's `#match?`) does not support backreferences, so the predicate can't enforce that the bound variable in `case _ as X` matches the one passed to `assert_never(X)`. Relax to `\w+` for both occurrences — false negatives only occur for code that no one writes in practice.
2. Verify during implementation that `#not-match?` evaluates against multi-line node text in tree-sitter 0.22. If it doesn't, the fallback is a new `PostFilter::MatchExhaustiveness` variant in `src/rules/ast/ast_rule.rs` (~20 lines, mirroring the existing `ClassNameNotException`). This is the one place in the sculptor port where a small code change might be needed.

Acceptance:
- 1 new TOML file (and possibly one PostFilter variant if needed).
- cargo nextest run / cargo clippy / cargo fmt --check all pass.

title: Investigate no-inline-functions undercount vs sculptor (delta -5)
description:
Sculptor's Python ratchet `inline_functions` counts 27 violations. Our library's `no-inline-functions` counts 22 on the same codebase. Delta -5.

## Sculptor's rule

Defined at `imbue_core/imbue_core/ratchets/ratchet_rules.py`, name `inline_functions`. Implementation:

```python
FullFileRatchetTest(
    name="inline_functions",
    regex=re.compile(
        r"(?<!@staticmethod\n)(?<!@abstractmethod\n)^[ \t]+def\s+\w+\((?!\s*(?:#[^\n]*\n)?\s*(?:cls|self)\b)",
        flags=re.MULTILINE,
    ),
    exclude_test_files=True,
    ...
)
```

In English: any `def NAME(` that is indented AND whose first arg isn't `cls`/`self` (allowing for an optional comment line). Decorated `@staticmethod`/`@abstractmethod` cases are excluded via lookbehind. Test files excluded.

So sculptor catches: function definitions inside other functions, plus methods of classes whose first arg isn't `self`/`cls` (unusual but possible).

## Our rule

`builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-inline-functions.toml`:

```toml
query = """
(function_definition
  body: (block
    (function_definition) @violation))
"""
exclude = "@python_tests"
```

Catches: function definitions nested inside the body block of another function definition. Strictly "function-inside-function" — does not catch methods with non-self first args (which sculptor would catch).

## Hypothesis

The 5-count gap is real and could be either of:
1. Our query misses methods whose first arg isn't `cls`/`self` (sculptor catches these, we don't). Possibly 5 such cases in sculptor.
2. Our query catches the function-in-function case correctly; sculptor catches a broader set due to regex matching on indented `def`. Sculptor catches what we miss.

It's also possible sculptor catches false positives our query correctly skips.

## Investigation

1. Run our pilot at `/Users/danver/imbue/sculptor` to enumerate the 22 cases we catch.
2. Run sculptor's `python -m imbue_core.ratchets.test_all_ratchets test` or use sculptor's `get_most_recently_broken_ratchets.py` to enumerate the 27 sculptor catches.
3. Diff the two sets. The 5 lines sculptor catches that we don't — what shape are they? Methods with weird first args? Decorated functions our query missed? Real function-in-function we're missing structurally?
4. Decide: tighten our query, or document why our scope differs.

## Acceptance

EITHER:
- Tighten our tree-sitter query to catch the missing 5 cases. Re-run pilot — `inline_functions` counts should match. Add an integration test in `tests/sculptor_group_c_tests.rs` covering the previously-missed shape.
- OR: Demonstrate with concrete evidence that the 5 sculptor catches are not actual policy violations (e.g. they're decorated methods sculptor's regex's lookbehind doesn't fully exclude, or they're inside paths we correctly exclude but sculptor doesn't). High burden of proof.

`cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy`, `cargo nextest run`, `cargo run --release -- check` all pass. Atomic commit, trailer `Co-authored-by: Sculptor <sculptor@imbue.com>`.

Do NOT touch `.beads/` — coordinator handles bead state.

title: Strip leading ./ from file paths before glob include/exclude matching
description:
When `ratchets check` is invoked with no path argument or `.`, the file walker emits paths with a `./` prefix (e.g. `./sculptor/frontend/src/App.tsx`). The `applies_to_file` check in `RegexRule` and `AstRule` then passes that path verbatim to `globset::GlobSet::is_match`, and the leading `./` makes anchored globs like `sculptor/frontend/src/**/*.tsx` silently fail to match.

Repro:

```
RATCHETS=target/release/ratchets
TMP=$(mktemp -d)
mkdir -p "$TMP/sculptor/frontend/src"
echo '<button>X</button>' > "$TMP/sculptor/frontend/src/App.tsx"
cat > "$TMP/ratchets.toml" <<'TOML'
[ratchets]
version = "1"
languages = ["typescript"]
TOML
cd "$TMP" && $RATCHETS check --format jsonl | grep raw-html
# (empty)
cd "$TMP" && $RATCHETS check sculptor --format jsonl | grep raw-html
# {"type":"summary","rule":"no-raw-html-button","region":".","violations":1,"budget":0,"status":"fail"}
```

## Impact (from sculptor parity pilot)

Seven Group B rules silently report 0 violations against sculptor's codebase when they should report nonzero (matches sculptor's Python ratchet counts):
- `no-raw-html-button` (sculptor: 17, ours: 0)
- `no-integration-page-evaluate` (25 / 0)
- `no-integration-time-sleep` (7 / 0)
- `no-integration-page-reload` (4 / 0)
- `no-sculptor-subprocess` (2 / 0)
- `no-sculptor-copytree` (1 / 0)
- `no-integration-non-testid-queries` (1 / 0)

Plus the same bug likely explains two large positive deltas where Group C rules using `exclude = "@python_tests"` overcount because the test-file exclude doesn't strip `./` either:
- `no-underscore-imports`: 66 ours vs 4 sculptor (test files not excluded)
- `no-untyped-args-kwargs`: 33 ours vs 14 sculptor (same)

This is a blocker for sculptor migration. The library reports MORE violations than it should for some rules (false positives) and silently MISSES violations for others (false negatives), both as a function of where you invoke it from.

## Suggested fix

The path comparison is done in two places: `applies_to_file` on `RegexRule` (src/rules/regex_rule.rs) and on `AstRule` (src/rules/ast/ast_rule.rs). Normalize the path before the `is_match` call — strip a leading `./` if present. A small helper in `src/rules/rule.rs` would be cleanest since both rule types need it.

Also consider whether the file walker itself should emit normalized paths (so other consumers — e.g. JSONL output, region resolver — see canonical paths). The trade-off is that the violation `file:` field in output would no longer match the verbatim walker emission.

## Acceptance

- Repro above resolves: `ratchets check --format jsonl` from the temp dir reports `no-raw-html-button` violations.
- New integration test that exercises `include` glob matching when invoked from the parent dir of a configured-region subdirectory.
- The sculptor parity pilot (see `https://github.com/imbue-ai/ratchets/pull/10` followup, or run `target/release/ratchets check` against `/Users/danver/imbue/sculptor`) gets the seven Group B rules to fire and the two @python_tests excludes to actually exclude.
- cargo nextest run / cargo clippy / cargo fmt --check all pass.

title: Investigate no-typing-builtin-imports undercount vs sculptor (delta -3)
description:
Sculptor's Python ratchet `no_unnecessary_typing_imports` counts 3 violations against its codebase. This library's existing builtin `no-typing-builtin-imports` counts 0 on the same codebase.

## Sculptor's rule

Defined at `imbue_core/imbue_core/ratchets/ratchet_rules.py` (rule name `no_unnecessary_typing_imports`):

```python
regex=re.compile(r"from typing import \b(Dict|Tuple|List|Set|Optional)"),
```

Catches `from typing import Dict`, `from typing import Tuple`, etc., AND `from typing import Optional`.

## Our rule

`builtin-ratchets/python/regex/no-typing-builtin-imports.toml`:

```toml
pattern = "\\bfrom\\s+typing\\s+import\\s+.*\\b(Dict|List|Set|Tuple)\\b"
```

Catches Dict, List, Set, Tuple — but NOT Optional.

## Hypothesis

Most likely: sculptor's 3 violations are `from typing import Optional` statements. Adding `Optional` to our pattern (and likely `Tuple` is fine — sculptor has Tuple too) would close the gap.

## Investigation

1. Read sculptor's pilot config at `/Users/danver/imbue/sculptor/ratchets.toml`, `.ratchetignore`. The pilot binary is built from this repo at `target/release/ratchets`.
2. Run our library against sculptor and dump per-violation records (use `ratchets check --verbose --format jsonl` and grep for `no-typing-builtin-imports`).
3. In parallel, grep sculptor's codebase directly for `from typing import.*Optional` outside excluded paths to enumerate the 3 expected violations.
4. Confirm whether the 3 are all Optional imports or some other case.

## Acceptance

EITHER:
- Fix our `no-typing-builtin-imports` pattern to include `Optional` (and any other type sculptor catches that we miss). After fix, re-run pilot — `no_unnecessary_typing_imports` counts should match exactly. Bump ratchet-counts.toml for the project's own ratchets if registration introduces substrings that trip other rules.
- OR: Demonstrate with concrete evidence that sculptor's 3 violations are spurious (e.g. they're inside paths sculptor's exclude logic doesn't catch but ours correctly does, or they're false positives in sculptor's regex). The burden of proof is high — show the exact files and rule logic.

`cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features`, `cargo nextest run` (within 120s), `cargo run --release -- check` all pass. Atomic commit, trailer `Co-authored-by: Sculptor <sculptor@imbue.com>`.

Do NOT touch `.beads/` — coordinator handles bead state.

title: Investigate no-untyped-args-kwargs overcount vs sculptor (delta +19)
description:
Sculptor's `args_kwargs` counts 14 violations. Our `no-untyped-args-kwargs` counts 33. Delta +19.

## Sculptor's rule

`imbue_core/imbue_core/ratchets/ratchet_rules.py`, name `args_kwargs`:

```python
regex=re.compile(r"(def \w+\(.*\*args(?!: P\.args))|(def \w+\(.*\*\*kwargs(?!: P\.kwargs))"),
```

In English: any `def NAME(...*args` that isn't typed `*args: P.args`, OR `def NAME(...**kwargs` that isn't typed `**kwargs: P.kwargs`. Catches both untyped and incorrectly-typed splats. Critically: `.*` doesn't span newlines, so sculptor only catches single-line function signatures.

No `exclude_test_files`. Applies to all Python files.

## Our rule

`builtin-ratchets/python/ast/no-untyped-args-kwargs.toml`:

```toml
query = """
(function_definition
  parameters: (parameters
    [(list_splat_pattern) (dictionary_splat_pattern)] @violation))
"""
```

(Whatever shape we landed — read the file. Likely catches all `*args`/`**kwargs` patterns regardless of typing, or catches the splat-pattern node which is structurally untyped vs typed.)

## Hypothesis

Several possibilities — investigate which:

1. **Multi-line signatures**: tree-sitter happily traverses multi-line parameter lists; sculptor's regex stops at newlines. Sculptor MISSES untyped splats in functions whose signature spans multiple lines. The 19 extra ours catches are real violations sculptor under-counts.

2. **`P.args` / `P.kwargs` detection**: our query may not be excluding the legitimate paramspec cases. If our query catches `*args: P.args`, that's 19 false positives.

3. **Tree-sitter shape mismatch**: maybe our query catches a node that includes both untyped and typed cases without discrimination. Predicate filter missing.

## Investigation

1. Read our `no-untyped-args-kwargs.toml` query.
2. Run pilot against sculptor and dump the 33 violations with file paths and line numbers.
3. Diff against sculptor's 14 violations. The 19 extra — multi-line signatures? Or false positives where the splat IS typed as `P.args`/`P.kwargs`?
4. If real (multi-line), our query is correctly broader than sculptor's regex. If false positives (P.args/P.kwargs not excluded), tighten the query.

## Acceptance

EITHER:
- Tighten our query to properly exclude `P.args`/`P.kwargs` cases. Re-run pilot — counts should converge. Add tests covering both typed and untyped splats.
- OR: Demonstrate with concrete evidence (file paths + line snippets) that all 19 extra violations are real untyped splats that sculptor's single-line regex misses. The library is then strictly more correct than sculptor's regex; document the divergence in the rule's TOML description.

`cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy`, `cargo nextest run`, `cargo run --release -- check` all pass. Atomic commit, trailer `Co-authored-by: Sculptor <sculptor@imbue.com>`.

Do NOT touch `.beads/` — coordinator handles bead state.

title: Add ratchets check --since <ref> for diff-aware checking
description:
Sculptor's Python ratchets has a developer-facing helper `get_ratchets_broken_in_files_i_touched.py` that diffs the working tree against `origin/main`, then runs ratchets only on the changed files. This is how sculptor developers see what their PR breaks without scanning the whole codebase — a critical DX flow during migration.

Add a `--since <ref>` flag to `ratchets check` that gives the same workflow:

- New flag on `Cli` / `Command::Check` in `src/cli/args.rs`.
- When set, shell out to `git diff <ref> --name-only` (or use `git2` if already a dependency — it isn't currently; `std::process::Command` is fine) and intersect the result with the file walker's output. Equivalently: pass the changed-file list as the `paths` argument after gitignore/glob/language filtering.
- Belongs roughly in `src/cli/check.rs` and `src/cli/common.rs` (file discovery helpers).
- Edge cases: missing ref (clear error message; exit code 2), non-git directory (clear error message; exit code 2), files deleted in HEAD vs the ref (skip them silently).
- Integration test using a temp git repo with two branches.

Out of scope: the recency/blame ranking that sculptor's `get_most_recently_broken_ratchets.py` does. That's a separate feature.

Acceptance:
- `ratchets check --since <ref>` runs only against files changed since the ref.
- Integration test using a temp git repo passes.
- README.md documents the flag.
- cargo nextest run / cargo clippy / cargo fmt --check all pass.
